critique.summary: The article argues against the notion that coding is becoming obsolete due to AI advancements, emphasizing that coding remains essential for mastering complexity through abstraction. It highlights the challenges of precise specifications, especially in complex systems like collaborative text editors, and envisions AI and future AGI as tools to enhance, not replace, coding practices.
mainPoints: English specifications feel precise but are often vague until implemented and tested.
AI assists in converting English descriptions into running code, enabling iterative refinement.
"Vibe coding" creates an illusion of precision but breaks down at scale or complexity.
Collaborative text editors are fundamentally complex and difficult to implement correctly.
Abstraction is crucial for managing complexity in programming, allowing humans to handle more than 7±2 concepts at once.
AI and future AGI will help solve harder abstraction problems, improving code quality rather than making coding obsolete.
Code itself is an important artifact, akin to poetry, and not just a means to produce software.
The idea that coding is dead is misguided, similar to thinking storytelling ended with the printing press.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the challenges of precise specifications and the complexity of collaborative text editors are well-supported by known software engineering principles and historical difficulties in real-time collaboration software. The emphasis on abstraction aligns with established cognitive science and programming theory. The perspective on AI and AGI as tools to enhance coding rather than replace it reflects mainstream expert opinion and current technological trends. However, some claims, such as the future capabilities of AGI, remain speculative but are presented as opinions rather than facts. The article does not cite multiple external sources or perspectives explicitly, limiting its journalistic rigor.
considerations: The article does not provide empirical data or references to studies supporting the difficulty of collaborative text editors or the effectiveness of AI in coding.
It lacks perspectives from other experts or counterarguments about AI potentially reducing the need for traditional coding skills.
The timeline and impact of AGI remain speculative and uncertain, which should be acknowledged more explicitly.
The article assumes familiarity with programming concepts and does not define terms like 'vibe coding' or 'functional reactive programming' for a general audience.
Potential ethical and societal implications of AI and AGI in software development are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the evolving role of coding in the age of AI, debating whether AI can truly innovate or just replicate existing knowledge. Many acknowledge AI's utility in automating routine tasks but emphasize the continued need for human expertise, critical thinking, and code review to ensure quality and innovation. Concerns about vendor lock-in, comprehension debt, and the limits of AI-generated code are also raised.

comments.author: lateforwork
comments.text:
Chris Lattner, inventor of the Swift programming language recently took a look at a compiler entirely written by Claude AI. Lattner found nothing innovative in the code generated by AI [1]. And this is why humans will be needed to advance the state of the art.

AI tends to accept conventional wisdom. Because of this, it struggles with genuine critical thinking and cannot independently advance the state of the art.

AI systems are trained on vast bodies of human work and generate answers near the center of existing thought. A human might occasionally step back and question conventional wisdom, but AI systems do not do this on their own. They align with consensus rather than challenge it. As a result, they cannot independently push knowledge forward. Humans can innovate with help from AI, but AI still requires human direction.

You can prod AI systems to think critically, but they tend to revert to the mean. When a conversation moves away from consensus thinking, you can feel the system pulling back toward the safe middle.

As Apple’s “Think Different” campaign in the late 90s put it: the people crazy enough to think they can change the world are the ones who do—the misfits, the rebels, the troublemakers, the round pegs in square holes, the ones who see things differently. AI is none of that. AI is a conformist. That is its strength, and that is its weakness.

[1] https://www.modular.com/blog/the-claude-c-compiler-what-it-r...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and well-supported perspective on AI's limitations in innovation, citing an expert's evaluation and explaining why human direction remains crucial.

comments.author: jama211
comments.text:
The biggest point everyone keeps missing is that a single code review makes your vibe coded code go from “terrifyingly dangerous” to “better than most people’s code” in one step.

We’re at a point where LLMs write great code, way better than my average coworkers used to anyway. Of course, not reviewing said code by an expert would be a silly as not reviewing a coworkers code, there might be security vulnerabilities in there, hardcoded api keys, etc. But once it’s been professionally reviewed, it’s just as safe as any code written by a human only probably if a higher quality than most people write.

On HN there’s an argument I keep seeing go back and forth which is like “vibe coding is the worst thing ever” and the other side will be like “AI is the second coming of christ and we don’t need programmers” - I think the reason we have what appears to be such opposing views is that those views are actually really close to one another, and proper review is all that separates one from the other.

If you’re already an expert and you don’t vibe code most things and then carefully test and review after, you’re wasting the benefits of these machines. If you’re not an expert then you shouldn’t be employed in the first place, as the main thing people are employed for is responsibility, not output.

This has always been the way in everything. A foreperson gets paid more than a worker on a building site not because they build more than the worker, but because they’re responsible for more than the worker. This is the real reason why programmer jobs won’t go away in my opinion.
comments.analysis: The comment highlights the importance of human oversight in AI-generated code, balancing optimism about AI capabilities with practical concerns about security and quality.

comments.author: eichin
comments.text: Got flashbacks to 1999 from some of those charts - I had a pair of design charts (partly for arguments, partly for onboarding) that were 17 nodes each and a lot of lines. (A coworker snuck in some extra nodes and an arrow labeled "troops move through Austria" and it was a while before anyone other than me noticed - yeah, that kind of chart.) This is not a lesson in design complexity - the design was pretty tight for what it did, even if you go back and read the patents - it's a lesson in the use of abstraction for explanation complexity and that you can break up the presentation more sanely than the code-on-disk actually is, you just have to stop and think about it (and have a bit more empathy for the people you're presenting to than, well, anyone in 1999 actually had :-)
comments.analysis: This comment offers historical context and insight into the challenges of complexity in design and communication, relevant to understanding AI's role in coding.

comments.author: evanmoran
comments.text:
I’m writing a new type of CRDT that supports move/reorder/remove ops within a tree structure without tombstones. Claude Code is great at writing some of the code but it keeps adding tombstones back to my remove ops because “research requires tombstones for correctness”.

This is true for a usual approach, but the whole reason I’m writing the CRDT is to avoid these tombstones! Anyway, a long story short, I did eventually convince Claude I was right, but to do it I basically had to write a structural proof to show clear ordering and forward progression in all cases. And even then compaction tends to reset it. There are a lot of subtleties these systems don’t quite have yet.
comments.analysis: Provides a concrete example of AI's current limitations in understanding nuanced technical innovations, supporting the argument that human expertise is still necessary.

comments.author: vicchenai
comments.text: the integration glue comment really resonates. ive been using agents mostly for wiring up oauth flows and api integrations between services - stuff where theres no creativity involved, just reading 3 different docs and getting the tokens right. saved me hours on stuff i used to dread. but the moment i need to think about actual architecture decisions or tradeoffs, im back to my own brain. feels like thats where things will settle for a while.
comments.analysis: This pragmatic view aligns with many others that AI excels at automating repetitive tasks but cannot yet replace human judgment in complex decisions.

comments.author: ssowonny
comments.text: the moment your vibe-coded bot hits edge cases in message threading, you need someone who actually understands the abstraction layer.
comments.analysis: A concise reminder that AI-generated code requires human intervention for handling complex or unexpected scenarios.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
From "code" to "no-code" to "vibe coding" and back to "code".

What you are seeing here is that many are attempting to take shortcuts to building production-grade maintainable software with AI and now realizing that they have built their software on terrible architecture only to throw it away, rewriting it with now no-one truly understanding the code or can explain it.

We have a term for that already and it is called "comprehension debt". [0]

With the rise of over-reliance of agents, you will see "engineers" unable to explain technical decisions and will admit to having zero knowledge of what the agent has done.

This is exactly happening to engineers at AWS with Kiro causing outages [1] and now requiring engineers to manually review AI changes [2] (which slows them down even with AI).

[0] https://addyosmani.com/blog/comprehension-debt/

[1] https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2026/feb/20/amazon-cl...

[2] https://www.ft.com/content/7cab4ec7-4712-4137-b602-119a44f77...
comments.analysis: This comment warns about the risks of blindly trusting AI-generated code without sufficient human understanding, supported by real-world examples.

comments.author: _pdp_
comments.text:
Remember Deep Thought, the greatest computer ever built that spent 7.5 million years computing the Answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything? The answer was 42, perfectly correct, utterly useless because nobody understood the question they were asking.

That's what happens when you hand everything to a machine without understanding the problem yourself.

AI can give you correct answers all day long, but if you don't understand what you're building, you'll end up just like the people of Magrathea, staring at 42 and wondering what to do with it.

True understanding is indistinguishable from doing.
comments.analysis: Effectively highlights the difference between correctness and meaningful understanding, emphasizing the irreplaceable role of human cognition.

comments.author: randcraw
comments.text:
Krouse points to a great article by Simon Willison who proposes that the killer role for vibe coding (hopefully) will be to make code better and not just faster.

By generating prototypes that are based on different design models each end product can be assessed for specific criteria like code readability, reliability, or fault tolerance and then quickly be revised repeatedly to serve these ends better. No longer would the victory dance of vibe coding be simply "It ran!" or "Look how quickly I built it!".
comments.analysis: Offers a constructive vision of how AI can enhance software development by focusing on quality attributes rather than just rapid delivery.

comments.text:
I don't expect AI to replace me anytime soon, but...

AI is already letting me care less about the languages I use and focus more on the algorithms. AI helps me write tests. AI suggests improvements and catches bugs before compiling. AI writes helper scripts/tools for me. All of these things are good enough for me to accept paying a few hundred dollars every month, although I don't have to because my employer already does do that for me.

6 months ago I was arguing that AI wasn't very good and code was more precise than english for specifying solutions. The first part is not true anymore for many things I care about. The second is still true but for many things I care about it doesn't matter.

I'm getting tired of articles that try to tell me what to think about AI. "AI is great and will replace all programmers!"... "AI sucks and will ruin your brain and codebase!"... both of these are tired and meaningless arguments.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced personal experience with AI tools, acknowledging both their utility and the fatigue from extreme opinions in the discourse.

comments.author: soumyaskartha
comments.text:
Every few years something is going to kill code and here we are.
 The job changes, it does not disappear.
comments.analysis: A succinct reminder that technological shifts transform rather than eliminate programming roles.

comments.author: flitzofolov
comments.text:
r0ml's third law states that: “Any distributed system based on exchanging data will be replaced by a system based on exchanging programs.”

I believe the same pattern is inevitable for these higher level abstractions and interfaces to generate computer instructions. The language use must ultimately conform to a rigid syntax, and produce a deterministic result, a.k.a. "code".

Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h5fmhYc4U-Y
comments.analysis: Presents a theoretical perspective on the inevitability of code as the foundation of computing despite higher-level abstractions.

comments.author: neversupervised
comments.text: The author’s intuition is still backward calibrated, even though he talks about the future. He doesn’t have an intuition for the future. All code will be AI generated. There’s no way to compete with the AI. And whatever new downsides this brings will be solved in ways we aren’t fully anticipating. But the solution is not to walk back vibecoding. You have to be blind to believe not most code will be vibecoded very soon.
comments.analysis: This comment is overly deterministic and speculative, underestimating the complexity of software development and human roles.

comments.author: lionkor
comments.text:
To all the vibe coders:

When you let an LLM author code, it takes ownership of that code (in the engineering sense).

When you're done spending millions on tokens, years of development, prompt fine tuning, model fine tuning, and made the AI vendor the fattest wad of cash ever seen, you know what the vendor will do?

You have to migration path. Your Codex prompts don't work the same in Claude. All the prompts you developed and saved in commits, all the (probably proprietary) memory the AI vendor saved in their servers to make the AI lock you in even more, all of it is worthless without the vendor.

You are inventing "ah heck, we need to pay the consultant another 300 bucks an hour to take a look at this, because nobody else owns this code", but supercharged.

You're locking yourself in, to a single vendor, to such a degree that they can just hold your code hostage.

Now sure, OpenAI would NEVER do this, because they're all just doing good for humanity. Sure. What if they go out of business? Or discontinue the model that works for you, and the new ones just don't quite respond the same to your company's well established workflows?
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about dependency on AI vendors and the risks of losing control over AI-generated code.

comments.author: pacman128
comments.text: In a chat bot coding world, how do we ever progress to new technologies? The AI has been trained on numerous people's previous work. If there is no prior art, for say a new language or framework, the AI models will struggle. How will the vast amounts of new training data they require ever be generated if there is not a critical mass of developers?
comments.analysis: Highlights a realistic limitation of AI models relying on existing data and the chicken-and-egg problem for novel innovations.
